narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and an LSAT score of 27, you've put real effort into building your profile for law school, including planning to attend full-time and coming from a middle-income family background. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicted a 69.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the key threshold used to evaluate applications like yours—anything above around 50% tips the decision toward rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction stemmed primarily from your academic decile rankings in school, especially your Year 3 performance, which placed you in the bottom decile compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing. That lower Year 3 decile had the strongest pull toward the failure prediction, signaling to the model a potential struggle with sustained high-level coursework leading into bar prep. Your Year 1 decile in the second percentile added further concern, pushing the prediction in the same direction, though less intensely, as it highlighted an early pattern below the average fifth or sixth decile most students achieve. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, nudging the model toward expecting bar exam success since it's close to the dataset average of 3.21. Your LSAT score provided a small positive counterbalance too, despite being below the typical 36.7, and your full-time enrollment plans and middle-income background had minimal but slightly favorable influences. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your deciles stand out as notably lower, while your other metrics align more closely with averages—it's that gap in school performance that weighed heaviest. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-class family; it's the model prioritizing patterns from past bar takers where early and late school deciles strongly predict outcomes. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits or retakes if possible, consider LSAT retakes to push closer to 37+, and gather strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience. Reapply after addressing these, perhaps with additional undergrad-level courses to demonstrate upward trends. You've got the foundation—many have turned similar profiles around with persistence. Keep pushing; law school could still be yours.
